ops.title: Browser read fan-out with main-level priming
ops.body:
# Browser read fan-out with main-level priming

The browser read (gather.md `## Browser read`) fans out one gatherer subagent per host, but only AFTER a main-level priming step. This ordering is a hard invariant, not a style choice.

**Why priming must precede the fan-out:** every `cookiesync` call in a Claude Code session shares one Touch ID grant (the CLI derives the requestor from the session — see the agent-browser-with-cookies skill's "One session, one tap" note). A single `cookiesync auth --reason "getaway: read award balances and elite status from <host1>, <host2>, …"` at the main level costs the one tap and primes the grant for every subsequent call. **Unprimed concurrent `cookies` calls race:** N parallel gatherers firing before any grant exists can each raise their own Touch ID prompt.

**The consent property rides the priming tap:** the `--reason` names every host verbatim, so the user approves one informed, task-specific request covering the whole read. Per-host pulls after priming must not carry vague or divergent reasons. Touch ID denied at priming skips the entire browser read.

**After priming:** one gatherer per host, all spawned in a single message; each pulls only its own host (`cookiesync cookies <host>`) into its own named `agent-browser --session <slug>` session. Per-session grants are shared across the fan-out, so per-host pulls cost no extra taps. Failure branches are per-gatherer and non-blocking — one hung or logged-out host never stalls the others.

**History:** the read was previously one serial subagent visiting each site in turn, justified by "one cookie pull = one tap" — void, since the grant is per-session, not per-call. Changed in 1f6e1e703538. Per-host pulls also reduce exposure to cookiesync's dead-peer SSH stall stacking past its watchdog on large union pulls.
ops.when: Before refactoring the browser read flow or parallelizing cookiesync-based operations
